---
title: "Bayesian Econometrics"
subtitle: "Inference as belief updating"
---

## Overview

Everything in the book so far has been frequentist: parameters are fixed unknowns, and probability describes the behaviour of estimators across hypothetical repeated samples. Bayesian econometrics adopts a different and, to many, more intuitive stance: the parameter is uncertain, that uncertainty is described by a probability distribution, and data update prior beliefs into a posterior via Bayes' rule.

This module builds the Bayesian approach from first principles. It starts with priors, likelihoods, and posteriors --- and the conjugate cases where updating is closed-form and the posterior mean is a transparent compromise between prior and data (the same shrinkage that drives multilevel models). It then tackles the computational engine that makes modern Bayes practical: Markov chain Monte Carlo, with the diagnostics needed to trust it. It closes with Bayesian regression and hierarchical models, where partial pooling, regularization (ridge and LASSO as Gaussian and Laplace priors), and full uncertainty propagation emerge naturally --- a fitting capstone that reconnects to shrinkage, multilevel modelling, and the whole arc of the book.

## Pages in This Module

- [Bayesian Inference: Priors and Posteriors](bayes-basics.qmd) --- Updating beliefs with data.
- [MCMC: Sampling the Posterior](mcmc.qmd) --- When you cannot write the posterior down.
- [Bayesian Regression and Hierarchical Models](bayesian-regression-hierarchical.qmd) --- Priors, shrinkage, and pooling, done Bayesianly.
